fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each thread contains its own program counter (PC) and a private set of registers. They do NOT contain their own address space like a process.
Similar to the process control block (PCB), in order to perform a context switch each process needs a thread control block(TCB).
Additionally, the process needs one stack per-thread. This does ruin our neatly partitioned address space. However, it is generally OK because each individual stack is not too large.

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: within a process, there are often highly parallelizable components. However, we do not want to have to divide the program into processes and perform the much more expensive context switch.
Avoid blocking I/O: even if you do not want to parallelize your program you can still use a thread to avoid blocking I/O by just having that thread blocked while you continue doing other work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Suppose we have the following sequence of instructions (pseudo-assembly) mov addr reg add 1 reg mov reg addr If you have two threads running this and hope the end result is that you increment the corresponding variable by two, then you might be in for some disappointment. This is a non-atomic operation because conceivably
thread 1 could store the value of the register
thread 2 could store the value of the register
thread 1 increments
thread 2 increments
end result is only 1 increment This is a race condition and code that is accessed by multiple threads is the critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each thread contains its own program counter (PC) and a private set of registers. They do NOT contain their own address space like a process.
Similar to the process control block (PCB), in order to perform a context switch each process needs a thread control block(TCB).
Additionally, the process needs one stack per-thread. This does ruin our neatly partitioned address space. However, it is generally OK because each individual stack is not too large.

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: within a process, there are often highly parallelizable components. However, we do not want to have to divide the program into processes and perform the much more expensive context switch.
Avoid blocking I/O: even if you do not want to parallelize your program you can still use a thread to avoid blocking I/O by just having that thread blocked while you continue doing other work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Suppose we have the following sequence of instructions (pseudo-assembly) mov addr reg add 1 reg mov reg addr If you have two threads running this and hope the end result is that you increment the corresponding variable by two, then you might be in for some disappointment. This is a non-atomic operation because conceivably
thread 1 could store the value of the register
thread 2 could store the value of the register
thread 1 increments
thread 2 increments
end result is only 1 increment This is a race condition and code that is accessed by multiple threads is the critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while(lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

html:ul.html:li: Still have O(n) coherence traffic on release.
Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set next slot to "go-ahead"

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the locks. Padding to 64b can get expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Lock is a pointer to a node
html:ul.html:pre: acquire(lock): I->next = null; predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock,I) if predecessor != nil //some node holds lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I //predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait //spin till lock is free release(lock): if (I->next == null) //no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock,I,nil) //make sure… return //CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next = nil //spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false //wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P
procedure central_barrier if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P else repeat until count == P

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: When count == p one thread can reach the next barrier and decrement count again. Then all other threads will be stuck.

Each thread gets local "sense" variable. global (shared) count : integer := P global (shared) sense : bool := true global (private) local_sense : bool = false
procedure central_barrier local_sense = !local_sense if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P sense = local_sense else repeat until sense == local_sense
